Alright, your back, let's go right into lesson number one. Now here's the deal. I don't know about you, but growing up in my house with my mommy and my daddy always said to me, don't judge a book by its cover, right? Now let me ask you an honest question. I assume you were told the same thing too. Not just by your parents, but probably also by your school teachers. So here's the question. Do you judge books by their covers? Yes or no? Just be honest here. Okay, fair enough. I'm glad you think you don't, but here's the deal. You actually do judge a book by its cover. It's not because you're evil or you're a loathsome individual. It's basically a human thing. We all do it. Boom. I'm glad you're being asked. Of course you judge a book by its cover. It's basically a human thing. We all do it. You see, for better or worse, we human beings were basically fear-based creatures. We're constantly sizing up our surroundings and we make these snap decision based on how we perceive them. Is it safe? Is there danger nearby? Do we need to be extra careful about something? We need to run in line. Now this type of snap decision making strategy goes all the way back to our caveman days and it's wired directly into our reptilian brain. See, when we saw something back then, we had to instantly size it up and decide whether or not to stay or run. It was only after we sized things up and we were sure that we were safe that we start debating whether or not to stick around for some potential benefit. Now what you need to understand here is that this quick decision making instinctive strategy is still with us today. The stakes are much lower, of course. We're not getting chased around by saber-toothed tigers anymore, but the process itself happens just as quickly. In fact, it happens less than four seconds over the phone and only a quarter of a second when you're in person. That's how fast the brain reacts. Think about that for a second. It takes only a quarter of a second for a prospect to make a decision about you if the sale is in person. Now we know this because scientists have conducted experiments where they hook people up to a certain type of MRI machine called the PET scanner, which shows how the brain works as it's processing information. In fact, let's put a picture of a brain up right here. A lot of some fun, right? When they flash a test subject to picture, their brain's visual cortex lights up almost instantly, followed a quarter of a second later by their prefrontal lobe over here, which is where the brain's judgment center is located and where decisions get made. That's how quickly it happens. Now when the sales over the phone, you have a bit longer. You have four seconds to make that all important first impression. Now, just to be clear, even when you're in person, it still takes four seconds before the final judgment gets made. The only difference here is that the process starts sooner when you're in person. From the moment your prospect lays eyes on you. And then you have those four seconds until they essentially rip you apart, compartmentalize each piece, and then judge them and put it back together based on how you were perceived. And here's the bottom line. Whether you're in person or over the phone, there are three things that you absolutely must establish in the first four seconds if you want to be perceived in exactly the right way. And here's what they are. In those first four seconds, you must be perceived as being number one, sharp as a tack, number two, enthusiastic as hell, and number three, an expert in your field, a force to be reckoned with. And we repeat that. In those first four seconds of an encounter, you must be perceived as being number one, sharp as a tack, number two, enthusiastic as hell, and number three, an expert in your field, a force to be reckoned with. There's three absolutely crucial things must line up in the first four seconds of a sale. Otherwise, you set yourself for a massive uphill battle. Now in truth, you may be able to have another five or 10 seconds to play catch-up wall if you screw up those first four seconds, but after that, you're done. You cannot influence anybody. So now taking this back to not judging a book by its cover, well, if you thought you didn't, well, it's a very admirable quality for sure. If you put yourself inside one of those pet skinners, they have a sneaky suspicion that the results would surprise you. See bottom line again, the simple facts that we all judge books by their covers. And that includes my parents, my schoolteachers, despite their well-intentioned advice to do the opposite. It's basically hardwired into our brains. And just to be clear, this is not an American thing alone. It's also an Australian thing, a Chinese thing, a Brazilian thing. It's an Italian thing. It's basically a human thing. It exists everywhere in the world and it crosses all cultural boundaries. The bottom line is you have four seconds until someone rips you apart, componentializes each piece, judges them, and then puts you back together based on how you're perceived. And if those three A4 mentioned things, sharp as a tack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field, if they haven't been established firmly in your prospects mind, you have basically no shot of closing them. Now, why is that? Well, think about it for a second here. You really want to do business with a novice? I mean, when you go on by a car or a truck or a stock or a computer or an insurance policy, who do you want guiding you through the process, a novice or an expert? An expert, of course. In fact, we've been conditioned since we're basically yay big to seek out experts to help us solve all problems and eliminate our pain. When we were sick as kids, our parents took us to a doctor and we saw how we've been in our own parents who had deferred to this person. And when he asked us questions, we'd answer them without hesitation because our parents had explained to us that doctors were very special people. They'd been through years of schooling and were taught everything there was to know about making sick people feel better. And while you might have realized it at that time, everything how they were dressed, to how they talk to you, to how they asked questions, even a little black bitch to carry out for house calls, by the way, back in the olden days, it was all part of why you had competent them and even a single glance and why that even made you feel better just by being in their presence. These were true experts in their field. So much so, in fact, they even got to have special license plates on their claws, the pocket for the fire hydrants in the United States, which was even the police deferred to them. But that was only the beginning. As we got older, the conditioning continued. If we were struggling in school, our parents were hired as a tutor. We played a certain sport and hired as a coach. We entered adulthood, we picked up right where our parents left off, seeking out expert after expert to solve our problems, plan our future, and we'll teach our children the exact same thing. Think about it for a moment. I mean, if you were a scholar at Johansson and you were getting your hair cut for the Oscars, who would you seek out? A person that was just out of beauty school or the person who's been the top stylist in Hollywood for celebrities who've absolutely fabulous for the last 20 years? You get it? And who do you think Roger Federer turned to back in 2015 to break out of his so-called slump and he wanted to be five more majors? Did he hire a local tennis pro or was it a former number one player in the world themselves who had a history of being a great coach? Bottom line, we all seek out pros or experts. We want to deal with people who are sharp on the ball and are enthusiastic about what they do. See, experts have a way of talking that literally commands respect. They say things like, listen, Bill, I've been doing this for quite some time now. I understand your problem and here's exactly what you need to do. Nobbuses on the other hand, well, they tend to speak in far less definite terms and their limited grasp on the deeper nuances of their product or industry becomes more and more apparent as the process continues. They move the prospect down the straight line and they have to actually free for meaning they run of stripped of material. At that point, they're basically done. It becomes apparent that they're total nobbuses. My point here is that how you're perceived will carry you to every part of the sale, but it starts in the first four seconds. If you screw that up, make a negative first impression, you basically have no shot of closing the deal. That's why it's so absolutely critical that you establish those three crucial things in the first four seconds of the sale. Number one, your sharpest attack, meaning you're smart and insightful, you're totally on the ball. You're basically a born problem solver who doesn't waste their time or yours. Number two, your enthusiastic sale, meaning enthusiasm is oozing out at every pore of your body. So, your prospect gets an overwhelming sense that whatever you're selling simply must be good. That's what real enthusiasm does it. Bypass is the logic center of your prospects brain and goes straight to their gut. That's the power of real enthusiasm and the operative word here is real. See what I'm not talking about is that crazy brand of over the top enthusiasm where you're yelling and screaming and flitting around and saying, oh my God, my product is so great. That's nonsense. Not only is that ridiculous in fact, but it's also the quickest way to get your client running for the exit. I'm talking about something totally different. It's bottled enthusiasm, which is basically just below the surface and really bubbling over as you speak. It's about enunciating your words with absolute clarity, stressing your confidence so your words have intensity to them. It's like you're talking with your fist clenched and there's a seeding volcano inside of you. It's ready to erupt at any moment, but of course it doesn't because you're an expert who's in total control. That type of bottled enthusiasm is truly one of the earmarks of number three here now, which is sounding like an expert in your field. That you're an expert, an authority figure, a force to be reckoned with. Bottom line is this. Your office attack, enthusiastic as hell, an expert in your field, when you've established those three things in your prospect's mind, they chunk up to one undeniable thing, namely that you are a person worth listening to and then one level higher still because you can help them achieve their goals. You can help them get what they want in life. In other words, your prospect won't listen to you so you can get what you want in life, which is to close a sale. They listen to get what they want to solve a problem, eliminate a pain point. You get it? Now I want to dig into this a bit more deeply in the next lesson, but let's stop right here. I want you to take a quick quiz and then meet me right back in the next lesson. I'll see you there.
